literal: settled-purport, the upshot, what-it-comes-to; from tat 'that' + para 'highest, ultimate'
invariant_definition: The settled purport of a text, determined by the convergent application of contextual indicators (the Mīmāṃsā six *liṅgas*). *Tātparya* is what the text is fundamentally about; it organizes the interpretation of every individual statement. *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4 (*tat tu samanvayāt*) defends the thesis that *brahman* is the *tātparya* of all *śruti*; this thesis is contested by Mīmāṃsā (which holds *karma* the *tātparya* of *karma-kāṇḍa*) and refined by all Vedāntic schools (each holding that its identification of *brahman* — *nirguṇa*, *saviśeṣa*, Viṣṇu, Kṛṣṇa — is the *tātparya*).

per_school.definition: *Tātparya* of *śruti* is *brahman*; *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.4 establishes this against Mīmāṃsā. School-internal differences on what 'brahman' refers to (see *brahman* entry) translate into school-internal differences on *tātparya*-content.
primary_loci: Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 1.1.4 (Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva — all comment on this sūtra)

translator_note: 'Settled purport' or 'overall import' acceptable. The Sanskrit term has no single English equivalent.
